mechanism: Proposed mechanism: HCA has been studied for effects on appetite and body weight; specific mechanisms in humans are not established clearly enough to treat as confirmed (product composition and co-ingredients vary).
activeCompounds: Hydroxycitric acid (HCA; "garcinia acid") is a key characterized constituent of the fruit rind in supplement products.
safetyNotes: NCCIH flags potential unsafe use due to reported liver damage cases (including severe cases), plus gastrointestinal adverse effects (e.g., nausea, diarrhea) and limited pregnancy/lactation data; LiverTox describes weight-loss products labeled as containing garcinia as linked to clinically apparent acute liver injury, sometimes severe/fatal.
description: A tree native to India / South Asia; preparations in supplements commonly emphasize the fruit rind, which contains hydroxycitric acid (HCA). Historically used as a food preservative and in folk medicine (e.g., inflammation and stomach complaints).
dosage: No direct dosage data. Contextual inference: Appetite suppressant; metabolism enhancer. Herbs often act via neurotransmitter modulation; antioxidant effects; or enzyme inhibition.. Contextual inference: Appetite suppressant; metabolism enhancer. none.; nan.
interactions: Reported interactions include concerns with drugs affecting the liver and serotonergic drugs; theoretical interaction potential is also noted, so medication review is advised.
legalStatus: Legal but subject to regulatory review
preparation: Commonly sold as extracts of the fruit rind and multi-ingredient weight-loss products containing garcinia extract/HCA.
region: Native range is Western India (accepted species *G. gummi-gutta*).
summary: Garcinia (hydroxycitric-acid–containing fruit rind extract) is marketed for appetite control and weight loss, but human evidence for meaningful weight-loss benefit is inconsistent; there are multiple reports of clinically apparent liver injury associated with products labeled as containing garcinia, including severe cases (uncommon overall).
